text: The surgeon performs a robotic right hemicolectomy, targeting the hepatic flexure. Bipolar forceps, scissors, and a tip-up clamp are positioned on the robotic arms, with the camera providing a clear view. The right colon is lifted to expose the alocholic vessels, and the heliocolic vessels are identified for dissection using a medial to lateral approach. The aneocolic vein and artery are clipped with amalocs and cut at their origin. A swab is placed cranially after completing the medial dissection. The mesentery of the distal ileum is divided using a robotic endo-vessel sealer, and lateral dissection follows the embryologic plane.

The surgeon divides adhesions between the omentum and transverse colon, revealing a tattoo marking. The omentum is further divided, and a swab placed after medial dissection is visible, protecting the duodenum and vessels. Following the completion of lateral dissection and the takedown of the hepatic flexure, the mesocolon is transected using a vessel sealer. A DII colectomy is performed, with the ileocolic artery and vein sectioned at their origin at the superior mesenteric vein and artery. The right colic artery and vein are absent, and the right branch of the middle colic vein and artery are sectioned at their origin.

The surgeon completes the dissection and divides the transverse colon and terminal ileum using an endostepla, positioned in the third robotic arm. The lymphoadipose tissue covering the superior mesenteric vein and artery, as well as the middle colic vessels, is removed.

The video displays the trochanter placement for robotic right kidney surgery, marked in red, contrasting with the placement used in this case, marked in blue. This setup facilitates performing both procedures without altering the patient's position or trochanter placement.

The surgeon performs dissection and release of the second adrenal portion to expose the renal pedicle. The renal pedicle is dissected to identify the right renal artery, which is prepared for clamping. The inferior pole of the kidney is exposed, and the margins of the lesion are marked with diatomy. The renal artery is clamped, initiating warm ischemia.

The surgeon performs a tumorectomy, ensuring macroscopically healthy tissue margins. A hemostatic continuous barbed suture is applied using Hemolox to reduce kidney worm ischemia. The arterial clamp is removed, and the kidney is checked for correct reperfusion and absence of active bleeding. The specimen is placed in a bag, and a hemostatic agent is applied at the tumorectomy site.

The surgeon performs a side-to-side isoperistaltic ileocolic anastomosis, using indocyanine green to assess the vascularization of the ileum and colon. The robotic setup includes bipolar forceps in the first arm, a needle holder in the third, a tip-up in the fourth, and the camera in the second arm. The posterior reinforcement is completed with a running absorbable suture, followed by the creation of two enterotomies. A staple side-to-side anastomosis is executed, and the defect is closed with absorbable stitches. An anterior reinforcement is applied using a barbed suture, and the epiprone covers the anastomosis.
The surgeon extracts both specimens placed in separate endo bags through a funnestine incision. The final pathology results are displayed.
